CSE5313CSE5313 Coding and information theory for data science (Lecture 22)

CSE5313 Coding and information theory for data science (Lecture 22)

Approximate Gradient Coding

Exact gradient computation and approximate gradient computation

In the previous formulation, the gradient ∑ivi\sum_i v_i∑i​vi​ is computed exactly.

  • Accurate
  • Requires d≥s+1d \geq s + 1d≥s+1 (high replication factor).
  • Need to know sss in advance!

However:

  • Approximate gradient computation are very common!
    • E.g., stochastic gradient descent.
  • Machine learning is inherently inaccurate.
    • Relies on biased data, unverified assumptions about model, etc.

Idea: If we relax the exact computation requirement, can have d<s+1d < s + 1d<s+1?

  • No fixed sss anymore.

Approximate computation:

  • Exact computation: ∇≜v=∑ivi=(1,⋯ ,1)v1,⋯ ,vn⊤\nabla \triangleq v = \sum_i v_i = (1, \cdots, 1) v_1, \cdots, v_n^\top∇≜v=∑i​vi​=(1,⋯,1)v1​,⋯,vn⊤​. ⊤.
  • Approximate computation: ∇≜v=∑ivi≈uv1,⋯ ,vn⊤\nabla \triangleq v = \sum_i v_i \approx u v_1, \cdots, v_n^\top∇≜v=∑i​vi​≈uv1​,⋯,vn⊤​,
    • Where d2(u,I)d_2(u, \mathbb{I})d2​(u,I) is “small” (d2(u,v)=∑i(ui−vi)2d_2(u, v) =\sqrt{ \sum_i (u_i - v_i)^2}d2​(u,v)=∑i​(ui​−vi​)2​).
    • Why?
  • Lemma: Let vu=u(v1,⋯ ,vn)⊤v_u = u (v_1, \cdots, v_n)^\topvu​=u(v1​,⋯,vn​)⊤. If d2(u,I)≤ϵd_2(u, \mathbb{I}) \leq \epsilond2​(u,I)≤ϵ then d2(v,vu)≤ϵ⋅ℓspec(V)d_2(v, v_u) \leq \epsilon \cdot \ell_{spec}(V)d2​(v,vu​)≤ϵ⋅ℓspec​(V).
    • VVV is the matrix whose rows are the viv_ivi​‘s.
    • ℓspec\ell_{spec}ℓspec​ is the spectral norm (positive sqrt of maximum eigenvalue of V⊤VV^\top VV⊤V).
  • Idea: Distribute S1,⋯ ,SnS_1, \cdots, S_nS1​,⋯,Sn​ as before, and
    • as the master gets more and more responses,
    • it can reconstruct uv1,⋯ ,vn⊤u v_1, \cdots, v_n^\topuv1​,⋯,vn⊤​,
    • such that d2(u,ℓ)d_2(u, \ell)d2​(u,ℓ) gets smaller and smaller.

[!NOTE] d≥s+1d \geq s + 1d≥s+1 no longer holds. sss no longer a parameter of the system, but s=n−#responsess = n - \#responsess=n−#responses at any given time.

Trivial Scheme

Off the bat, the “do nothing” approach:

  • Send SiS_iSi​ to worker iii, i.e., d=1d = 1d=1.
  • Worker iii replies with the iii‘th partial gradient viv_ivi​.
  • The master averages up all the responses.

How good is that?

  • For u=nn−s⋅Iu = \frac{n}{n-s} \cdot \mathbb{I}u=n−sn​⋅I, the factor nn−s\frac{n}{n-s}n−sn​ corrects the 1n\frac{1}{n}n1​ in vi=1n⋅∇ on Siv_i = \frac{1}{n} \cdot \nabla \text{ on } S_ivi​=n1​⋅∇ on Si​.
  • Is this ≈∑ivi\approx \sum_i v_i≈∑i​vi​? In other words, what is d2(nn−s⋅I,I)d_2(\frac{n}{n-s} \cdot \mathbb{I}, \mathbb{I})d2​(n−sn​⋅I,I)?

Trivial scheme: nn−s⋅I\frac{n}{n-s} \cdot \mathbb{I}n−sn​⋅I approximation.

Must do better than that!

Roadmap

  • Quick reminder from linear algebra.
    • Eigenvectors and orthogonality.
  • Quick reminder from graph theory.
    • Adjacency matrix of a graph.
  • Graph theoretic concept: expander graphs.
    • “Well connected” graphs.
    • Extensively studied.
  • An approximate gradient coding scheme from expander graphs.

Linear algebra - Reminder

  • Let A∈Rn×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}A∈Rn×n.
  • If Av=λvA v = \lambda vAv=λv then λ\lambdaλ is an eigenvalue and vvv is an eigenvector.
  • v1,⋯ ,vn∈Rnv_1, \cdots, v_n \in \mathbb{R}^nv1​,⋯,vn​∈Rn are orthonormal:
    • ∥vi∥2=1\|v_i\|_2 = 1∥vi​∥2​=1 for all iii.
    • vi⋅vj⊤=0v_i \cdot v_j^\top = 0vi​⋅vj⊤​=0 for all i≠ji \neq ji=j.
  • Nice property: ∥α1v1+⋯+αnvn∥22=∑iαi2\| \alpha_1 v_1 + \cdots + \alpha_n v_n \|_2^2 = \sqrt{\sum_i \alpha_i^2}∥α1​v1​+⋯+αn​vn​∥22​=∑i​αi2​​.
  • AAA is called symmetric if A=A⊤A = A^\topA=A⊤.
  • Theorem: A real and symmetric matrix has an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors.
    • That is, there exists an orthonormal basis v1,⋯ ,vnv_1, \cdots, v_nv1​,⋯,vn​ such that Avi=λiviA v_i = \lambda_i v_iAvi​=λi​vi​ for some λi\lambda_iλi​‘s.

Graph theory - Reminder

  • Undirected graph G=V,EG = V, EG=V,E.
  • VVV is a vertex set, usually n=1,2,⋯ ,nn = 1,2, \cdots, nn=1,2,⋯,n.
  • E⊆(V2)E \subseteq \binom{V}{2}E⊆(2V​) is an edge set (i.e., EEE is a collection of subsets of VVV of size two).
  • Each edge e∈Ee \in Ee∈E is of the form e=(a,b)e = (a, b)e=(a,b) for some distinct a,b∈Va, b \in Va,b∈V.
  • Spectral graph theory:
    • Analyze properties of graphs (combinatorial object) using matrices (algebraic object).
    • Specifically, for a graph GGG let AG∈{0,1}n×nA_G \in \{0,1\}^{n \times n}AG​∈{0,1}n×n be the adjacency matrix of GGG.
    • Ai,j=1A_{i,j} = 1Ai,j​=1 if and only if {i,j}∈E\{i,j\} \in E{i,j}∈E (otherwise 0).
    • AAA is real and symmetric.
    • Therefore, has an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors.

Some nice properties of adjacency matrices

  • Let G=(V,E)G = (V, E)G=(V,E) be ddd-regular, with adjacency matrix AGA_GAG​ whose (real) eigenvalues λ1≥⋯≥λn\lambda_1 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_nλ1​≥⋯≥λn​.
  • Some theorems:
    • λ1=d\lambda_1 = dλ1​=d.
    • λn≥−d\lambda_n \geq -dλn​≥−d, equality if and only if GGG is bipartite.
    • AGI⊤=λ1I⊤=dI⊤A_G \mathbb{I}^\top = \lambda_1 \mathbb{I}^\top = d \mathbb{I}^\topAG​I⊤=λ1​I⊤=dI⊤ (easy to show!).
      • Does that ring a bell? ;)
    • If λ1=λ2\lambda_1 = \lambda_2λ1​=λ2​ then GGG is not connected.

Expander graphs - Intuition.

  • An important family of graphs.
  • Multiple applications in:
    • Algorithms, complexity theory, error correcting codes, etc.
  • Intuition: A graph is called an expander if there are no “lonely small sets” of nodes.
  • Every set of at most n/2n/2n/2 nodes is “well connected” to the remaining nodes in the graph.
  • A bit more formally:
    • An infinite family of graphs GnG_nGn​ n=1n=1n=1 ∞\infty∞ (where GnG_nGn​ has nnn nodes) is called an expander family, if the “minimal connectedness” of small sets in GnG_nGn​ does not go to zero with nnn.

Expander graphs - Definitions.

  • All graphs in this lecture are ddd-regular, i.e., all nodes have the same degree ddd.
  • For sets of nodes S,T⊆VS, T \subseteq VS,T⊆V, let E(S,T)E(S, T)E(S,T) be the set of edges between SSS and TTT. I.e., E(S,T)={(i,j)∈E∣i∈S and j∈T}E(S, T) = \{(i,j) \in E | i \in S \text{ and } j \in T\}E(S,T)={(i,j)∈E∣i∈S and j∈T}.
  • For a set of nodes SSS let:
    • Sc=n∖SS^c = n \setminus SSc=n∖S be its complement.
    • Let ∂S=E(S,Sc)\partial S = E(S, S^c)∂S=E(S,Sc) be the boundary of SSS.
    • I.e., the set of edges between SSS and its complement ScS^cSc.
  • The expansion parameter hGh_GhG​ of GGG is:
    • I.e., how many edges leave SSS, relative to its size.
    • How “well connected” SSS is to the remaining nodes.

[!NOTE] hG=min⁡S⊆V,∣S∣≤n/2∂S∣S∣h_G = \min_{S \subseteq V, |S| \leq n/2} \frac{\partial S}{|S|}hG​=minS⊆V,∣S∣≤n/2​∣S∣∂S​.

  • An infinite family of ddd-regular graphs (Gn)n=1∞(G_n)_{n=1}^\infty(Gn​)n=1∞​ (where GnG_nGn​ has nnn nodes) is called an expander family if h(Gn)≥ϵh(G_n) \geq \epsilonh(Gn​)≥ϵ for all nnn.
    • Same ddd and same ϵ\epsilonϵ for all nnn.
  • Expander families with large ϵ\epsilonϵ are hard to build explicitly.
  • Example: (Lubotsky, Philips and Sarnak ‘88)
    • V=ZpV = \mathbb{Z}_pV=Zp​ (prime).
    • Connect xxx to x+1,x−1,x−1x + 1, x - 1, x^{-1}x+1,x−1,x−1.
    • d=3d = 3d=3, very small ϵ\epsilonϵ.
  • However, random graphs are expanders with high probability.

Expander graphs - Eigenvalues

  • There is a strong connection between the expansion parameter of a graph and the eigenvalues λ1≥⋯≥λn\lambda_1 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_nλ1​≥⋯≥λn​ of its adjacency matrix.
  • Some theorems (no proof):
    • d−λ22≤hG≤2d(d−λ2)\frac{d-\lambda_2}{2} \leq h_G \leq \sqrt{2d (d - \lambda_2)}2d−λ2​​≤hG​≤2d(d−λ2​)​.
    • d−λ2d - \lambda_2d−λ2​ is called the spectral gap of GGG.
      • If the spectral gap is large, GGG is a good expander.
      • How large can it be?
    • Let λ=max⁡{∣λ2∣,∣λn∣}\lambda = \max \{|\lambda_2|, |\lambda_n|\}λ=max{∣λ2​∣,∣λn​∣}. Then λ≥2d−1−on(1)\lambda \geq 2 d - 1 - o_n(1)λ≥2d−1−on​(1). (Alon-Boppana Theorem).
    • Graphs which achieve the Alon-Boppana bound (i.e., λ≤2d−1\lambda \leq 2 d - 1λ≤2d−1) are called Ramanujan graphs.
      • The “best” expanders.
      • Some construction are known.
      • Efficient construction of Ramanujan graphs for all parameters is very recent (2016).

Approximate GC from Expander Graphs

Back to approximate gradient coding.

  • Let ddd be any replication parameter.
  • Let GGG be an expander graph (i.e., taken from an infinite expander family (Gn)n=1∞(G_n)_{n=1}^\infty(Gn​)n=1∞​)
    • With eigenvalues λ1≥⋯≥λn\lambda_1 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_nλ1​≥⋯≥λn​, and respective eigenvectors w1,⋯ ,wnw_1, \cdots, w_nw1​,⋯,wn​
    • Assume ∥w1∥2=∥w2∥2=⋯=∥wn∥2=1\|w_1\|_2 =\| w_2\|_2 = \cdots = \|w_n\|_2 = 1∥w1​∥2​=∥w2​∥2​=⋯=∥wn​∥2​=1, and wiwj⊤=0w_i w_j^\top = 0wi​wj⊤​=0 for all i≠ji \neq ji=j.
  • Let the gradient coding matrix B=1dAGB=\frac{1}{d} A_GB=d1​AG​.
    • The eigenvalues of BBB are μ1=1≥μ2≥⋯≥μn\mu_1 = 1\geq \mu_2 \geq \cdots \geq \mu_nμ1​=1≥μ2​≥⋯≥μn​. where μi=λid\mu_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{d}μi​=dλi​​.
    • Let μ=max⁡{∣μ2∣,∣μn∣}\mu = \max \{|\mu_2|, |\mu_n|\}μ=max{∣μ2​∣,∣μn​∣}.
    • ddd nonzero entries in each row ⇒\Rightarrow⇒ Replication factor ddd.
  • Claim: For any number of stragglers sss, we can get close to I\mathbb{I}I.
    • Much better than the trivial scheme.
    • Proximity is a function of ddd and λ\lambdaλ.
  • For every sss and any set K\mathcal{K}K of n−sn - sn−s responses, we build an “decoding vector”.
    • A function of sss and of the identities of the responding workers.
    • Will be used to linearly combine the n−sn - sn−s responses to get the approximate gradient.
  • Let wK∈Rnw_{\mathcal{K}} \in \mathbb{R}^nwK​∈Rn such that (wK)i={−1if i∉Ksn−sif i∈K(w_{\mathcal{K}})_i = \begin{cases} -1 & \text{if } i \notin \mathcal{K} \\ \frac{s}{n-s} & \text{if } i \in \mathcal{K} \end{cases}(wK​)i​={−1n−ss​​if i∈/Kif i∈K​.

Lemma 1: wKw_{\mathcal{K}}wK​ is spanned by w2,⋯ ,wnw_2, \cdots, w_nw2​,⋯,wn​, the n−1n - 1n−1 last eigenvectors of AGA_GAG​.

Proof

w2,⋯ ,wnw_2, \cdots, w_nw2​,⋯,wn​ are independent, and all orthogonal to w1=Iw_1 = \mathbb{I}w1​=I.

⇒\Rightarrow⇒ The span of w2,⋯ ,wnw_2, \cdots, w_nw2​,⋯,wn​ is exactly all vectors whose sum of entries is zero.

Sum of entries of wKw_{\mathcal{K}}wK​ is zero ⇒\Rightarrow⇒ wKw_{\mathcal{K}}wK​ is in their span.

Corollary: wK=α2w2+⋯+αnwnw_{\mathcal{K}} = \alpha_2 w_2 + \cdots + \alpha_n w_nwK​=α2​w2​+⋯+αn​wn​ for some αi\alpha_iαi​‘s in R\mathbb{R}R.

Lemma 2: From direct computation, the norm of wK=α2w2+⋯+αnwnw_{\mathcal{K}} = \alpha_2 w_2 + \cdots + \alpha_n w_nwK​=α2​w2​+⋯+αn​wn​ is nsn−s\frac{ns}{n-s}n−sns​.

Corollary: wK2=∑i=2nαi2=nsn−sw_{\mathcal{K}}^2 = \sum_{i=2}^n \alpha_i^2 = \frac{ns}{n-s}wK2​=∑i=2n​αi2​=n−sns​ (from Lemma 2 + orthonormality of w2,⋯ ,wnw_2, \cdots, w_nw2​,⋯,wn​).

The scheme:

  • If the set of responses is K\mathcal{K}K, the decoding vector is wK+ℓ2w_{\mathcal{K}} + \ell_2wK​+ℓ2​.
  • Notice that supp⁡(wK+ℓ2)=K\operatorname{supp}(w_{\mathcal{K}} + \ell_2) = \mathcal{K}supp(wK​+ℓ2​)=K.
  • The responses the master receives are the rows of Bv1,⋯ ,vn⊤B v_1, \cdots, v_n^\topBv1​,⋯,vn⊤​ indexed by K\mathcal{K}K.
  • ⇒\Rightarrow⇒ The master can compute wK+ℓ2Bv1,⋯ ,vn⊤w_{\mathcal{K}} + \ell_2 B v_1, \cdots, v_n^\topwK​+ℓ2​Bv1​,⋯,vn⊤​.

Left to show: How close is wK+ℓ2Bw_{\mathcal{K}} + \ell_2 BwK​+ℓ2​B to ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​?

Proof

Recall that:

  1. wK=α2w2+⋯+αnwnw_{\mathcal{K}} = \alpha_2 w_2 + \cdots + \alpha_n w_nwK​=α2​w2​+⋯+αn​wn​.
  2. wiw_iwi​’s are eigenvectors of AGA_GAG​ (with eigenvalues λi\lambda_iλi​) and of B=1dAGB = \frac{1}{d} A_GB=d1​AG​ (with eigenvalues μi=λid\mu_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{d}μi​=dλi​​).

d2(wK+IB,I)=d2(I+α2w2+⋯+αnwnB,I)d_2 (w_{\mathcal{K}} + \mathbb{I} B, \mathbb{I}) = d_2 (\mathbb{I} + \alpha_2 w_2 + \cdots + \alpha_n w_n B, \mathbb{I})d2​(wK​+IB,I)=d2​(I+α2​w2​+⋯+αn​wn​B,I) (from 1.)

d2(wK+IB,I)=d2(I+α2μ2w2+⋯+αnμnwn,I)d_2 (w_{\mathcal{K}} + \mathbb{I} B, \mathbb{I}) = d_2 (\mathbb{I} + \alpha_2 \mu_2 w_2 + \cdots + \alpha_n \mu_n w_n, \mathbb{I})d2​(wK​+IB,I)=d2​(I+α2​μ2​w2​+⋯+αn​μn​wn​,I) (eigenvalues of BBB, and μ1=1\mu_1 = 1μ1​=1)

d2(wK+IB,I)=d2(I+α2μ2w2+⋯+αnμnwn,I)d_2 (w_{\mathcal{K}} + \mathbb{I} B, \mathbb{I}) = d_2 (\mathbb{I} + \alpha_2 \mu_2 w_2 + \cdots + \alpha_n \mu_n w_n , \mathbb{I})d2​(wK​+IB,I)=d2​(I+α2​μ2​w2​+⋯+αn​μn​wn​,I) (by def.).

d2(wK+IB,I)=∥∑i=2nαiμiwi∥2d_2 (w_{\mathcal{K}} + \mathbb{I} B, \mathbb{I}) = \|\sum_{i=2}^n \alpha_i \mu_i w_i\|_2d2​(wK​+IB,I)=∥∑i=2n​αi​μi​wi​∥2​

d2(wK+IB,I)=∥∑i=2nαiμiwi∥2d_2 (w_{\mathcal{K}} + \mathbb{I} B, \mathbb{I}) = \|\sum_{i=2}^n \alpha_i \mu_i w_i\|_2d2​(wK​+IB,I)=∥∑i=2n​αi​μi​wi​∥2​ (w_i’s are orthonormal).

d2(wK+IB,I)=∑i=2nαi2μi2d_2 (w_{\mathcal{K}} + \mathbb{I} B, \mathbb{I}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=2}^n \alpha_i^2 \mu_i^2}d2​(wK​+IB,I)=∑i=2n​αi2​μi2​​ (w_i’s are orthonormal).

Improvement factor

Corollary: If B=1dAGB = \frac{1}{d} A_GB=d1​AG​ for a (d-regular) Ramanujan graph GGG,

  • ⇒\Rightarrow⇒ improvement factor ≈2d\approx \frac{2}{d}≈d2​.
  • Some explicit constructions of Ramanujan graphs (Lubotsky, Philips and Sarnak ‘88)
    • with 2 2d\frac{2}{d}d2​ ≈\approx≈ 0.5!

Recap

  • Expander graph: A ddd-regular graph with no lonely small subsets of nodes.
    • Every subset with ≤n/2\leq n/2≤n/2 has a large ratio ∂S/S\partial S / S∂S/S (not →0\rightarrow 0→0 with nnn).
    • Many constructions exist, random graph is expander w.h.p.
    • The expansion factor is determined by the spectral gap d−λ2d - \lambda_2d−λ2​,
    • Where λ=max⁡{λ2,λn}\lambda = \max \{\lambda_2, \lambda_n\}λ=max{λ2​,λn​}, and λ1=d≥λ2≥⋯≥λn\lambda_1 = d \geq \lambda_2 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_nλ1​=d≥λ2​≥⋯≥λn​ are the eigenvalues of AGA_GAG​.
    • “Best” expander = Ramanujan graph = has λ≤2d−1\lambda \leq 2 d - 1λ≤2d−1.
  • “Do nothing” approach: approximation nsn−s\frac{ns}{n-s}n−sns​.
  • Approximate gradient coding:
    • Send ddd subsets Sj1,⋯ ,SjdS_{j1}, \cdots, S_{jd}Sj1​,⋯,Sjd​ to each node iii, which returns a linear combination according to a coefficient matrix BBB.
    • Let B=1dAGB = \frac{1}{d} A_GB=d1​AG​, for GGG a Ramanujan graph: approximation λdnsn−s\frac{\lambda}{d} \frac{ns}{n-s}dλ​n−sns​.
    • Up to 50% closer than “do nothing”, at the price of higher computation load

[!NOTE] Faster = more computation load.
